forecast.title: Payroll Forecast
forecast.subtitle: 6 models evaluated — best performer selected automatically
forecast.ml_unavailable: ML service unavailable. Make sure the Python API is running on port 8000.
forecast.names_in_fr: Names shown in French (no EN translation available)
forecast.chart_sub: Monthly net payroll (TND) — last 48 months + forecast
forecast.seasonal_note: Seasonal pattern: May peaks (bonuses/raises) → summer stabilisation. This is expected behaviour, not a decline.
forecast.estimate_note: Forecast estimate = this segment's historical share × total forecast. Per-segment models were not trained; this is a proportional projection.
forecast.model_comparison_sub: All 6 models evaluated on the same 12-month hold-out test set. Hover model names for explanations.
forecast.model_hover_hint: Hover model names to learn how each works
forecast.table_note: Ridge was excluded: a MAPE of 0% on the test set indicates the model memorised training data (data leakage). SARIMA is selected as winner based on lowest MAPE on the 12-month hold-out test set.
forecast.employee_search_sub: Enter an employee ID to view their pay history and 6-month projection
forecast.emp_no_data: No payroll data found for this employee
forecast.emp_method_body: YoY growth rate of {{rate}}% applied to the last recorded pay. The 90% confidence interval is derived from the employee's pay variability (std of last 24 months).

note.recurring_may: This peak recurs every year around {{date}}
note.recurring_may_why: → Annual step increment and/or performance bonus — standard in the Tunisian public sector
note.recurring_nov: This peak recurs every November in your pay history
note.recurring_nov_why: → Year-end bonus paid regularly each November
note.recurring_generic: This month is consistently higher in your history
note.recurring_generic_why: → Recurring bonus or salary adjustment (avg {{pct}} above previous month)
note.onetime: One-time spike — pay dropped back the following month
note.onetime_why: → Likely a retroactive back-pay or one-off exceptional payment
note.persistent: Lasting raise — pay held at this level in subsequent months
note.persistent_big_why: → Likely a promotion, grade change, or salary reclassification
note.persistent_small_why: → Likely a step increment or category upgrade
note.generic_rise: Increase of {{pct}} compared to previous month
note.drop_after_spike: Return to normal level after last month's bonus
note.drop_after_spike_why: → The drop reflects the end of the exceptional payment
note.drop_persistent: Drop that persisted in subsequent months
note.drop_persistent_why: → Possible change in administrative status, recurring deduction,
note.drop_persistent_why2: or reduction of a standing allowance
note.drop_generic_why: → Likely an exceptional deduction, unpaid leave, or pay correction

forecast.peak_may: May peak — annual bonuses and step increments
forecast.above_median: Notable rise above the historical median
forecast.below_median: Month below the usual median level
forecast.feature_eng_sub: Input features built from pay history · RF/XGBoost winner shows ranked importances
forecast.feature_imp_na: Feature importance not available for {{model}} (statistical model — no tree structure).

ridge.label: Ridge Regression
ridge.desc: Linear model with L2 regularization. Assumes a straight-line relationship between lag features and payroll. Extremely fast but lacks the ability to capture seasonality. A perfect R² here signals overfitting — the model memorised the training set rather than learning true patterns, so it is excluded from winner selection.

rf.label: Random Forest
rf.desc: Ensemble of 100 decision trees, each trained on a random subset of the 12-month lag features, rolling averages, and seasonal signals. Robust to outliers and captures non-linear relationships. However, tree-based models cannot extrapolate beyond their training range, which limits accuracy for long-term upward trends.

xgb.label: XGBoost
xgb.desc: Gradient Boosting — sequentially builds weak learners where each corrects the errors of the previous. State-of-the-art on tabular data competitions. More prone to overfitting than Random Forest when the time series is short (< 200 points).

sarima.label: SARIMA
sarima.desc: Seasonal ARIMA — the gold standard for monthly time series. Explicitly models three components: trend (AR terms), integration (differencing for stationarity), and seasonality (SAR/SMA terms with period = 12 months). Highly interpretable and consistently reliable for payroll data. Selected as winner when MAPE is lowest.

prophet.label: Prophet (Meta)
prophet.desc: Open-source model by Meta (Facebook). Decomposes the series into trend + yearly seasonality + weekly seasonality + holidays. Robust to missing data and outliers. Well-suited for business forecasting but can be outperformed by SARIMA when data is clean and seasonal patterns are regular.

tft.label: TFT (Deep Learning)
tft.desc: Temporal Fusion Transformer — state-of-the-art deep learning model for multi-horizon time series. Uses multi-head self-attention to weight past time steps, gating mechanisms to select relevant features, and static covariate encoders. Requires large datasets (thousands of series) to outperform classical methods; on 136 monthly data points it tends to underfit.

MAPE.desc: Mean Absolute Percentage Error — average % error between predicted and actual. 5% means the model is off by 5% on average. Primary ranking metric. Lower is better.

SMAPE.desc: Symmetric MAPE — fixes a bias in regular MAPE: over-predicting and under-predicting by the same amount are penalised equally. More reliable when actual values vary widely. Lower is better.

MASE.desc: Mean Absolute Scaled Error — compares the model to a naïve seasonal baseline (just predicting last year's same month). MASE < 1 means the model beats that baseline. MASE = 1.1 means it's 10% worse than the naïve approach on the test year.

DA.desc: Directional Accuracy — % of months where the model correctly predicted whether payroll would go up or down. Critical for budget planning. 50% = coin flip, 100% = perfect direction.

MAE.desc: Mean Absolute Error — average error in TND. 26M TND means predictions deviate by ~26 million dinars per month on average. Same unit as payroll, easy to interpret.

RMSE.desc: Root Mean Square Error — like MAE but penalises large one-off mistakes more heavily. Useful for detecting models with occasional catastrophic errors.

anomalies.title: Anomaly Detection
anomalies.subtitle: Multi-method AI detection — Z-score, OCSVM, Isolation Forest & LSTM Autoencoder
anomalies.retrain_notice: Model retrain required — Rolling Z-score, detection method and temporal context will be available after running python -m ml.model_anomaly.
anomalies.sample_note: {{ total }} flagged from {{ records }} records · {{ employees }} employees

chatbot.name: INSAF AI Assistant
chatbot.subtitle: LLaMA 3.2 + RAG · Live payroll data · 16 intents
chatbot.greeting: How can I help you today?
chatbot.intro: Ask me anything about the Tunisian civil servant payroll — employees, salaries, grades, ministries, regions, anomalies, indemnities, trends, and forecasts.
chatbot.placeholder: Ask about payroll data… (Enter to send, Shift+Enter for newline)
chatbot.disclaimer: Answers are grounded in real DW data. Verify critical figures against source reports.
chatbot.ai_error: AI service unavailable. Make sure the Python API and Ollama are running.

hints.h1: How many employees are in the payroll?
hints.h2: What is the total net pay for 2024?
hints.h3: Which ministry has the highest payroll budget?
hints.h4: Show me the anomaly detection results
hints.h5: What is the average salary by grade?
hints.h6: How has the payroll evolved over the years?
hints.h8: Show salary distribution across all employees

reports.title: Reports & Analytics
reports.subtitle: Interactive Power BI dashboards for payroll intelligence
reports.not_conf_hint: Replace YOUR_REPORT_ID and YOUR_TENANT_ID in reports.component.ts with your actual Power BI embed URL.
reports.step1: Publish your report to Power BI Service
reports.step2: Go to File → Embed report → Website or portal
reports.step3: Copy the embed URL and paste it in the component

ingest.title: Data Ingestion
ingest.subtitle: Upload payroll files → ETL pipeline → Data Warehouse
ingest.choose_file: Choose how to provide the file
ingest.path_label: Full path to the data file on this machine
ingest.path_hint: Use this for large files (>1 GB). The API reads the file directly from disk — no HTTP transfer needed.
ingest.retrain_hint: All forecast & anomaly models will retrain (~2–5 min)
ingest.test_hint_pre: Pipeline will stop after the first
ingest.test_hint_post: matching rows — useful for validating the full stack.
ingest.keep_open_hint: The ETL pipeline is running on the server. Closing this tab won't stop the process, but you'll lose live progress tracking.
ingest.dw_reminder: Data Warehouse updated. Power BI will reflect changes on next scheduled refresh or manual refresh in the Reports page.
